root_cause: The '{match[1]}' script is not defined in package.json
fix: Add a '{match[1]}' entry to the 'scripts' block in your package.json
prevention: Keep package.json scripts in sync across branches and validate in CI

root_cause: npm registry is unreachable — likely a transient network issue in the CI runner
fix: Add retry logic: use 'npm install --prefer-offline' or configure '--registry https://registry.npmjs.org' explicitly. Re-running the workflow often resolves this.
prevention: Pin registry in .npmrc and add a retry step in your workflow

root_cause: Module '{match[1]}' is missing from node_modules — not installed or wrong import path
fix: Run 'npm install' and ensure '{match[1]}' is listed in dependencies or devDependencies. Check for typos in the import path.
prevention: Commit your package-lock.json and use 'npm ci' instead of 'npm install' in CI

root_cause: TypeScript compiler error TS{match[1]} — type mismatch or missing type definitions
fix: Run 'npx tsc --noEmit' locally to see the full error. Check if you need to install a '@types/*' package for the affected module.
prevention: Run 'tsc --noEmit' as a pre-commit hook to catch type errors before push

root_cause: Python package '{match[1]}' is not installed — missing from requirements.txt or not installed in CI
fix: Add '{match[1]}' to requirements.txt (with a pinned version) and run 'pip install -r requirements.txt' in your workflow before the failing step.
prevention: Use 'pip freeze > requirements.txt' to capture exact versions and cache pip in CI

pattern: Could not find a version that satisfies the requirement (\S+)
root_cause: No compatible version of '{match[1]}' exists for the current Python version or constraint set
fix: Relax the version pin for '{match[1]}' in requirements.txt or check PyPI for available versions compatible with your Python version.
prevention: Use 'pip-compile' (pip-tools) to generate a lockfile with fully resolved dependencies

pattern: COPY failed.*: file not found in build context
root_cause: A file or directory referenced in a COPY instruction does not exist relative to the Docker build context
fix: Check the COPY source path is relative to your build context (the directory passed to 'docker build'). Run 'docker build --no-cache .' locally to reproduce.
prevention: Add a .dockerignore file and test Docker builds locally before pushing

pattern: toomanyrequests.*docker\.io|You have reached your pull rate limit
root_cause: Docker Hub anonymous pull rate limit reached (100 pulls/6 hours per IP for unauthenticated requests)
fix: Add Docker Hub authentication to your workflow: create a secret DOCKERHUB_TOKEN and add a 'docker/login-action' step before any image pulls.
prevention: Always authenticate to Docker Hub in CI and consider mirroring base images to GitHub Container Registry

root_cause: The GitHub Actions workflow token lacks the required permission for this operation
fix: Add a 'permissions' block to your workflow YAML. For example: 'permissions: pull-requests: write' for PR comments, or 'contents: write' for pushing.
prevention: Always declare explicit permissions in workflow files — avoids relying on default token scopes

pattern: fatal: could not read Username for 'https://github.com'
root_cause: Git operations in this step are not authenticated — GITHUB_TOKEN is not being passed to the git credential helper
fix: Use 'actions/checkout' with 'token: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}' or set 'git config --global url.https://x-access-token:${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}@github.com/.insteadOf https://github.com/'
prevention: Always use 'actions/checkout' for git operations and pass GITHUB_TOKEN explicitly

root_cause: Environment variable '{match[1]}' is not set in this workflow run
fix: Add '{match[1]}' as a GitHub Actions secret: go to your repo → Settings → Secrets and variables → Actions → New repository secret. Then reference it in your workflow with '${{ secrets.{match[1]} }}'.
prevention: Document required secrets in your README and validate them at workflow start with a dedicated check step

root_cause: pytest collected zero test files — file naming or pytest.ini configuration doesn't match your test files
fix: Ensure test files follow pytest naming conventions: files must be named 'test_*.py' or '*_test.py'. Check your pytest.ini or pyproject.toml [tool.pytest.ini_options] testpaths setting.
prevention: Add a CI check that fails if 0 tests are collected: 'pytest --collect-only -q | grep -E "^[0-9]+ tests"'

pattern: jest.*no tests found|Your test suite must contain at least one test
root_cause: Jest found no test files matching your testMatch or testRegex configuration
fix: Check your jest.config.js 'testMatch' glob pattern. Default matches '**/__tests__/**/*.[jt]s?(x)' and '**/?(*.)+(spec|test).[jt]s?(x)'. Rename your test files to match.
prevention: Keep test file naming consistent and validate the config with 'jest --listTests' locally

root_cause: package-lock.json is missing or not committed — npm cannot perform a clean install
fix: Run 'npm install' locally to generate package-lock.json, then commit it. Use 'npm ci' instead of 'npm install' in CI for reproducible installs.
prevention: Never add package-lock.json to .gitignore and enforce 'npm ci' in all CI workflows

pattern: EADDRINUSE|address already in use|port (\d+) is already in use
root_cause: A port is already bound — likely a previous test run or service didn't shut down cleanly in CI
fix: Kill the process using the port before starting your service: 'npx kill-port {match[1]}' or use a dynamic port in tests.
prevention: Use 'server.close()' in test teardown and prefer random ports (port: 0) in integration tests
